subject: Chemistry
unit: Unit 3: Properties of Substances and Mixtures

learning_objectives.statement: Explain the relationship between the motion of particles and the macroscopic properties of gases with: i. The kinetic molecular theory (KMT). ii. A particulate model. iii. A graphical representation.

depth_profile: Students must relate the macroscopic properties of gases to the motions of particles using the kinetic molecular theory (KMT). They must understand that all particles in a sample are in continuous, random motion, and that the Kelvin temperature of a sample is directly proportional to the average kinetic energy of its particles. The topic requires the use of the equation KE = ½ mv² to relate average kinetic energy to average velocity. Furthermore, students must use Maxwell-Boltzmann distributions as graphical representations to describe the distribution of energies and velocities of particles at a given temperature.
assessment_notes: This topic is assessed via the 'Model Analysis' skill (4.A), requiring students to predict and/or explain chemical properties or phenomena using given chemical theories, models, and representations. Questions may ask students to interpret particulate models or graphical representations, specifically Maxwell-Boltzmann distributions, to explain gas behavior and particle velocities at different temperatures.

spec_checklist.can_do: The kinetic molecular theory (KMT) relates the macroscopic properties of gases to motions of the particles in the gas. The Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution describes the distribution of the kinetic energies of particles at a given temperature.
I can describe the distribution of kinetic energies of particles at a given temperature using a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution.

All the particles in a sample of matter are in continuous, random motion. The average kinetic energy of a particle is related to its average velocity by the equation:
EQN: KE = ½ mv2.
I can relate the average kinetic energy of a particle to its average velocity using the equation KE = ½ mv².
The Kelvin temperature of a sample of matter is proportional to the average kinetic energy of the particles in the sample.
The Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution provides a graphical representation of the energies/velocities of particles at a given temperature.
